data.title: American Eagle: Solid Value And Awesome Turnaround Potential
data.description:
One of my favorite technical-trading bottom formulas put out a clear buy signal on American Eagle Outfitters, Inc. (NYSE:AEO) yesterday, July 23rd, 2025. Retailing is a tough sector for investors, with changing fashions and consumer tastes plus a cutthroat competitive environment. Apparently, news that one of the most popular female acting stars in Hollywood, Sydney Sweeney, has jumped aboard the company's Fall back-to-school advertising campaign has caught Wall Street's attention. The business goal is to sell more high-dollar, high-profit margin jeans to youngsters. Well, the American Eagle homepage here is plastered with her picture in all types of jean products.

I am not a retail analyst. I don't know if the campaign will be a huge success or not. What I do know is that the AEO stock chart pattern is screaming buy to me. So, I have purchased a small starter position. Let me explain my logic for this.

Excellent Valuation Story

Shares are relatively cheap, getting an "A" Quant Factor Valuation Grade from Seeking Alpha's computer sorting system. The stock was trading just above book value, with a forecast P/E ratio under 10x next year's expected income result, before the latest announcement.

The forward 1-year earnings ratio of 9.6x is one of the lowest in the major apparel retailing group today. Interestingly, AEO has declined from one of the most expensive stocks on this financial metric to the opposite side of the spectrum for investors over the past 12 months.

When we include changing debt and cash positioning by the company, enterprise valuations are also close to bargain levels. EV to EBITDA around 4x and sales of 0.36x are in the bottom 25% and 5%, respectively, of long-term ranges over the last 20 years. In combination, only the early 2020 COVID pandemic shutdown of retail stores and the 2009 Great Recession generated lower valuations in recent memory.

At $11.30 per share, today's 9.9% free cash flow and 4.4% dividend yields don't hurt the investment appeal either. While neither is spectacular vs. other established retailers, both are well above average for the industry.

Short Squeeze Potential

The whole bullish argument is also helped by a large short interest position (a 30-month high in June), standing close to 13% of outstanding share counts, going into the news event. Sure, sales during 2025 to date have disappointed. Plus, the stock price has gone nowhere for a decade. Both have talked short sellers into confidently increasing their position size since early January, with total short interest doubling.

My investment take on the situation: we could see extensive short covering and Meme retail investor buying continue for weeks. I have witnessed more than a few huge stock gainers over the years after a celebrity name puts his/her weight behind a depressed stock. This could be a repeat bullish development.

Technical Chart Turnaround In July

Given shares have an impressive underpinning of value after a -60% price slide over the past 12 months into June's $9 price low, a sharp reversal higher cannot be ruled out. And, if the Sydney Sweeney news helps to spike company sales and profits unexpectedly, there is plenty of room for the stock quote to rebound 50%, maybe even 100%, back to last year's high level.

The first point to ponder on the technical trading chart is that the price has been trying to hold the April low level. American Eagle experienced major sales related to Wall Street fears of higher tariff costs on imported clothing (the business model). While the concern is definitely warranted, Asian inventory sourcing will be a headache for all competitors as well. The likely fix will be to pass along extra expenses through higher retail selling prices.

With the spike in price yesterday and today above its 50-day moving average, it appears something of a double bottom has been outlined in April and June. The good news is that regular and extensive selling volumes in the stock may have ended. Truly heavy volume days since early March (boxed in blue below) likely mean the average owner of AEO shares today is far different than when the new year began. It's possible strong-hand value buyers may not want to sell their shares until a retracement move plays out, with the price well above $11.

Other positives include accumulation patterns in the 20-day Chaikin Money Flow (circled in green) and 13-day Force Index (circled in purple) readings, in tandem during July, really for the first time in over a year.

Lastly, On Balance Volume has been zigzagging in the right direction since early June (boxed in gold), after a brutal decline from October. Taken together, I am quite bullish on the momentum pattern, confident enough that capitulation has taken place to reverse the price out of its downtrend. Followed and supported by months of basing in price, the next major move for American Eagle shares should be up. This assumes a stock market crash and/or slide into recession is not immediately ahead of us.

Another big reason I am buying AEO shares comes from my readout of the standard Renko chart setup. When we review the volume necessary to move price, heavy selling in June has been overcome in July (boxed in gold) with comparatively minor buying interest. To a degree, a vacuum of sellers may now exist. Translation: any new short covering and/or meme retail buying could push the price up quickly. The simple fact of improved sales trends at the operating business in the months ahead could also encourage extra share demand in the not-too-distant future. Such is the current supply/demand breakdown for the stock I like to watch closely.

Final Thoughts

Is American Eagle Outfitters a guaranteed winner? Absolutely not. My main operating business worry revolves around the decent odds of recession arriving in the second half of the year. However, customer excitement over Ms. Sweeney's promoted jeans may override a lackluster macroeconomic environment in the general retail outlook.

If sales do not rebound, it's entirely possible a serious recession sends the price under tangible book value, which is around $7 today. Yet, the odds of the stock staying below this level for more than several months look rather remote to me. The price-to-tangible BV multiple of 1.7x is already close to the lowest in trading history.

My thinking is that AEO's price around $10 may have already discounted the worst effects of Asian tariffs and a slowing U.S. economy (84% of FY2025 sales and 92% of long-lived assets in America). If this is true, not only could shares rise in price, but AEO could morph into a leading retail-industry investment performer through early 2026.

More good news for shareholders: management repurchased about 5% of outstanding ownership units, using cash on hand over the last quarter. This effort should leverage future price upside and "per share" results quite nicely for existing investors.

Given a "reversion-to-the-mean" investment outlook, a return trip to $15 or even $20 is possible over the next 6-12 months. Such would represent a normalized valuation zone on P/E, sales, cash flow, and book value, given only a minor blip higher for business results vs. past years.

In a best-case scenario, where surprising strength in sales is approaching, a price closer to $25 might be appropriate for a price target. A normalized valuation setup on a 10% or 15% jump in results over current expectations would get American Eagle to a double on your money (at $11.30 for a buy price) or more, when including the dividend payout over the next 12 months.

For sure, all of the above investment ingredients/variables deserve a Buy rating for American Eagle Outfitters. The upside reward potential appears to materially outweigh risks.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Betterware de Mexico Q2: Still Undervalued Despite A Strengthening Outlook
data.description:
Q2 results and full-year guidance for Betterware de México, S.A.P.I. de C.V. (BWMX) show a strong rebound for the business following a weak Q1, where revenue fell and margins declined, leading to negative FCF. The company returned to growth and FCF generation this quarter, with guidance pointing to an acceleration in revenue growth, coupled with margin expansion. Despite this, shares remain very cheaply valued, especially against relevant peers, offering a dividend yield of nearly 12%. I recommend staying long BWMX stock.

Key Takeaways From Q2 results

The company posted Q2 revenue and EBITDA, which were up 5.1% and 3.5% year over year, respectively. Amidst these results, the soft performance in the Betterware segment masks the strong performance from Jafra Mexico, where revenue and EBITDA growth were 10.9% and 14.2%, respectively, as shown below.

Jafra Mexico now contributes nearly 60% of the company's EBITDA, and its performance puts it ahead of U.S.-based beauty and personal care peers like L'Oreal (OTCPK:LRLCF) and Ulta Beauty (ULTA).

While I expect Jafra Mexico to be a key growth driver ahead, management appears to be making positive strides with respect to expanding to markets beyond Mexico, launching in Ecuador with a base of 2,500 associates. Though Ecuador's GDP is less than 7% of Mexico's, I believe the overall expansion plan which includes countries like Columbia and Peru, appears to be a significant growth opportunity.

Moving to FCF generation in Q2, it was up 29.2% year over year, reflecting a FCF conversion from adjusted EBITDA of 87.2%. This was mainly helped by inventory levels sequentially declining from Q1 by over 6%. Despite this, inventory levels were still 14.5% higher compared to Q2 2024, which implies that there is further room for inventory de-stocking, which should help lift FCF generation in the coming quarters.

Outlook And Risks To Consider

Management's latest guidance for the rest of the year implies that revenue growth is expected to accelerate to the low teens in the second half of FY25 versus the prior year period, with EBITDA expected to correspondingly increase by around 20%. While these expectations represent a sharp improvement compared to H1, it is notable that management is only assuming a stable macroeconomy, and not an improving one in their guidance, according to commentary on the call.

These improved growth expectations are founded on the strategic product introductions planned as well as the higher associate and distributor base at Betterware when exiting Q2. Additionally, Jafra Mexico continues to enjoy tailwinds from the Mexican beauty and personal care market that is forecasted to be growing at over a 5% CAGR for the coming years.

Turning to margins, a stronger Mexican Peso versus USD (MXN:USD) compared to H1 2025 should lead to lower import costs for goods. This, together with lower freight costs and less promotional activity, are what management attributes to expanding margins ahead.

When it comes to risks, it is important to acknowledge that demand for the company's products is sensitive to macroeconomic changes, a factor that we saw play out in Q1. Besides this, I believe the biggest risk for investors stems from the weak balance sheet, which is comprised of $243 million in net debt. In this regard, the strong FCF in Q2, along with expectations for continued strength in the second half, gives me confidence that leverage can decline over time from the current level where net debt-to-EBITDA stands at 1.97.

While geographic expansion into other Central American markets represents an attractive long-term growth opportunity, there is also the possibility that these expansions beyond Mexico are unsuccessful, much like the Betterware brand's earlier, unsuccessful attempt to enter the U.S. market. Nevertheless, I view the risk to be limited this time, given the much smaller scale of these markets and the lower capital requirements involved.

BWMX Stock Valuation

I expect FY25 FCF of $94 million, assuming a FCF conversion from EBITDA of 60%, at a currency exchange rate of 1 USD = 18.5 MXN. Based on BWMX's enterprise of $650 million, after accounting for its net debt of $243 million, shares are currently valued at an EV/FCF multiple of 6.9.

If I adjust for the temporary FCF headwinds in Q1 caused by higher taxes, normalized FCF conversion should be around 70%, which is close to historical levels, and implies a normalized FY25 FCF of $110 million. In this case, its EV/FCF valuation multiple drops to just 5.9, trading at just a fraction of the multiples assigned to relevant peers like L'Oreal and Ulta Beauty.

While a valuation discount may be warranted due to BWMX's concentration in the Mexican market and its reliance on the MLM model, I believe the current discount is too large and does not account for the business's positive aspects, such as its capital-light nature and diversification between the Betterware and Jafra brands.

Meanwhile, as investors await a potential multiple re-rating higher, they stand to benefit from the stock's massive FCF yield on equity, which stands at over 22%, while offering a dividend yield of nearly 12%.

BWMX Stock Remains A Buy

The performance for Jafra Mexico was particularly impressive in Q2, and the company's overall growth story remains intact, especially with the geographic expansion ahead. Margins remain healthy and are set to improve in the coming quarters, enabling strong FCF generation, which in turn should alleviate debt concerns. The cheap valuation and attractive dividend yield make BWMX stock still a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Paramount's Legacy Business Shrinks Faster Than Streaming Grows
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Paramount (NASDAQ:PARA) (NASDAQ:PARAA) is a stagnating company despite a growing streaming business. However, this is not growing fast enough to compensate for the declining business sector, which means that sales have tended to stagnate or fall for years, and content generation for streaming is eating up a lot of money. I just don't see enough evidence that the business will improve significantly, and given the stagnation, I don't think the valuation is that favorable.

This article will not talk about which new content they produce or the Skydance merger, as I think it was discussed enough in other articles. It is also not discussing a potential arbitrage play, which Seeking Alpha author Paul Franke did here very well. I want to focus on the big picture and the raw numbers. Unless otherwise stated, my articles are more focused on whether it is an attractive long-term investment. And as far as that is concerned, I have not built up enough confidence in Paramount after my analysis.

Let's start with the long-term chart.

It looks like a company in decline. The question is whether Paramount Global can make a successful turnaround. It almost seems like a race: Can the rise of the new streaming segment, Paramount+, make up for the decline of the legacy TV media segment? I'll try to find out in this article.

Industry Overview

Streaming is becoming more competitive as more companies are trying to enter this market. A few days ago, "Nielsen's Media Distributor Gauge" was released, where YouTube leads for the fifth time in a row and even continues to extend its lead, while Netflix records the biggest gain and enters the top 3 for the first time.

The Gauge™ is Nielsen's monthly snapshot of total broadcast, cable and streaming consumption that occurs through a television screen, providing the industry with a holistic look at what audiences are watching. (...)

while time spent watching streaming content in June was up over 5% compared to May, Netflix viewership surged 13.5% over the same period. (...)

YouTube also received a boost from school-aged audiences in June and remained atop the Media Distributor Gauge rankings for a fifth consecutive month. The streamer notched a 6% monthly viewing increase over May and represented 12.8% of TV usage, its largest share of television to date.

nielsen.com

To me, this is quite crazy: it is very expensive for companies like Paramount to enter the streaming market and produce the necessary content. Whereas Google spends nothing to produce content for YouTube, the users do it themselves, and just because they get a share of the revenue. And yet YouTube is much more popular overall.

Overall, the growth of subscription or ad-based video streaming seems far from over, and the US market is by far the biggest.

The streaming video revolution is still in full flower — with the sector poised for healthy growth over the next five years, according to the latest projections from PwC.

The U.S. total over-the-top (OTT) market is projected to increase at a 5.9% compound annual growth rate over the next five years — reaching $112.7 billion by 2029, according to this year's PwC Global Entertainment & Media Outlook report. That would be up 33% from the segment's $84.7 billion haul last year. The primary drivers of that are a growing subscriber base, new service launches and price increases, according to PwC's analysis.

variety.com

The Past: Financial Progress and Trends

First, an overview over a longer period for revenues, expenses, and free cash flow. I think it makes sense to look at this at the beginning to understand the big picture of where the company roughly stands. What we can see is stagnating revenue and free cash flow, which is only marginally positive and hasn't always been in every quarter.

In the chart above, it looks like the margin was much better around 2021. Below we see that margins were indeed stronger in 2021 and have mostly been on a downward trend since then. One exception is the improvement in gross profit margin from 2024, but this is not the most important figure, as it was improved by layoffs.

I created this income statement comparison for the last quarter compared to the previous year, showing where money is spent and how sales and costs are generally developing.

So, overall, pretty bad numbers, in my opinion. The most worrying thing for me is that revenue is down 6% while the cost of revenue is down much less in percentage terms. This has led to significantly worse gross profit and operating income. Net income has no year-on-year comparison as it was negative $5.4B in the previous year due to special effects.

Overall, I think the dividend is a good measure of the financial health of the company. A constant or rising amount of free cash would probably lead to stable or increasing dividends, but this is not the case. I also added the total dividends paid in dollars; you can see that since 2023 there is less and less money available for the dividend. That's okay, insofar as the health of the business is of course the top priority, but it doesn't paint a good picture in terms of business development. At least for me, when investing in a barely growing value company, I would like to have secure and preferably high dividends.

Paramount+

As I wrote at the beginning, it's like a race overall, where the rising revenue from the streaming division has to make up for the falling revenue from the TV Media division. But it's not happening; as a result, the company's total revenue is not increasing.

Looking closer at these numbers above and also the latest quarterly results per segment, we can see that the shrinking of TV Media happens faster than the rise of Direct-to-Consumer.

The company additionally explains:

Total company revenue decreased 6%, which included an 8 percentage point impact from CBS's broadcast of Super Bowl LVIII in the year ago period. Excluding the Super Bowl comparison, Q1 revenue grew 2%. Total company advertising revenue decreased 19%, reflecting a 19 percentage point impact from CBS's broadcast of Super Bowl LVIII in the year ago period. Excluding the comparison, Q1 advertising revenue was unchanged.

Q1 2025 results

But in my opinion, these explanations are not that important. In every research, I find an explanation for poor results. And in the next quarter, there are other explanations. In the charts above, we see a long-term decline in the TV Media segment, and the last quarter followed this trend; these are the hard facts that are more important than the explanations.

The Present: Valuation and Current Developments

The company is currently valued at an enterprise value of $22.5B; its market cap is $9.3B, which means there is net debt of around $13B.

In terms of valuation, we can look at different approaches and compare them with other companies. It is fair to say that the stock is relatively cheap-by far the cheapest in terms of forward P/E and EV/EBITDA. I think EBITDA is a useful metric for comparisons to show the ability to generate cash before taking into account interest payments or taxes. But Paramount is also the slowest-growing among these four.

A 5-year chart of the forward P/E ratio of the company separately, showing that it used to be even cheaper.

The Future: Outlook

What to expect looking forward? Overall, it is very hard to judge because it seems like the company is quite dependent on further rising subscriber numbers of its streaming segment, as the TV media segment will probably continue to decline; at least this is the long-term trend.

But like I mentioned earlier, the streaming segment is increasingly crowded, and the more existing subscribers, the more difficult it gets to grow this number even further. Especially when YouTube is still growing in popularity and Netflix is outspending Paramount by a wide margin.

Paramount+ revenue grew 33% in the year, 10 million new subscribers came to Paramount+ for a total of 77.5 million, and DTC profitability improved by nearly $1.2 billion. More importantly, more than a quarter of the company's revenue is now generated in our DTC segment, which positions us well for the future. And, with continued focus on strengthening our financial position, we also executed our stated goal of $500 million in annual run-rate cost savings.

June 2025 letter to stockholders

What we can do is to create a few different scenarios for possible future share prices. Let's take the estimates as a basis and use three different P/E ratios.

Year EPS estimate P/E 7 (Pessimistic) P/E 10 (base case) P/E 13 (optimistic)
2025 $1.26 $8.82 $12.60 $16.38
2026 $1.45 $10.15 $14.50 $18.85
2027 $1.43 $10.01 $14.30 $18.59

As I'm writing, the stock price is $13.26. So, assuming the EPS estimates will be accurate, the only attractive scenarios are those with higher P/E ratios, such as 13 in this case. If it remains at around 10, as it is now, the stock would remain relatively unchanged. An even lower P/E ratio would result in a heavy loss. To me, it looks like a stock owner would be quite dependent on a multiple expansion to make money, which is not an attractive scenario to be in.

It should also be noted that in the past, earnings revisions have tended to go down; so, analysts may gradually lower their estimates further, which will make the expected return even worse than in my table above.

All in all, these various scenarios seem rather unattractive to me, considering that although one segment of the company is growing, another is in slow decline. Overall, it is a stagnant company.

AI - Risk or Opportunity?

A big elephant in the room is the emergence of AI video, and I'm honestly not sure whether this is more of an opportunity or a risk for companies like Paramount, Disney (DIS), and Netflix (NFLX). On the one hand, they already have the brands and the user base. Their best-case scenario is to use AI video generation to greatly reduce costs and improve margins.

On the other hand, they don't own the AI models, so the same models would also enable numerous new players to enter the market. Up to now, there have been high entry barriers due to high costs. But if the production of films and series becomes considerably cheaper thanks to AI video, we can assume that numerous other competitors will enter the market in the future.

Overall, I expect a flood of new content in the future, which will mean that only the best of the best content will prevail. It remains to be seen to what extent all this will affect today's media companies. However, I believe there is a lot of potential for disruption from small, more flexible companies.

Share Dilution, Insider Trades & SBCs

Overall, all three aspects are fine: the outstanding share count is rising only slowly, SBCs are rather low, and I have not found any insider trades for the last six months.

Conclusion

Overall, I'm not convinced. The still largest core business is constantly shrinking, while the company is trying to gain a foothold in a competitive new segment where Netflix spends considerably more money on content. On the various charts, we saw that the shrinking is happening faster than the growth. In my view, there is simply too little tangible to suggest a positive share performance over the next few years (leaving aside the potential arbitrage play). I therefore rate the share as a sell, because that's what it is for me in the long term.

At the end of articles, I like to include my so-called investor checklist: a brief summary of some key points. In this case, I have to answer 5 out of 7 points negatively.

Value Checklist

Check

Why it's important

Rising revenues?

No

Looking for consistency and reliability

Improving margins?

No

Signals a possible competitive edge

Low PEG ratio?

No

PEG ratio below one may suggest undervaluation

Healthy balance sheet?

Questionable

Can the company absorb a downturn?

Rewards shareholders?

Slightly

Returning capital to shareholders

Shareholder negatives?

No

Excessive levels of SBCs, dilution, insider trades?

Stock in an uptrend?

No

Trading above its 200-day moving average?

data.title: Double-Checking The Credit Rating (Part 9): Pebblebrook Hotel Trust
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

In today's article, we will double-check the credit rating of Pebblebrook Hotel Trust (NYSE:PEB) and its financial instruments.

Company Overview

PEB was founded in 2007 and is headquartered in Maryland, US. The company specializes in investments in luxury hotels and full-service resorts located in or near urban centers of major metropolitan areas and key entry points into the United States. At the time of writing, it has 46 hotels and resorts, 13 urban and resort markets, and a current market capitalization of close to $1.3 billion. The total assets are around $5.65 billion.

In the picture below, you can see some of the hotel's properties:

The asset coverage ratio of PEB is 216%. The company earns about 16.30% gross on rental. The operating expenses, excluding depreciation and amortization, are approximately 79% of the total revenue. The interest expenses are around 4.33% of the debt. When calculating the asset yield (by dividing total revenues by total asset value), we obtain a value of 25.8%. However, for a more accurate assessment, a market-based adjustment is applied using the price-to-book value (P/B) ratio, which is currently 0.47. After the market adjustments, the calculated asset yield is 34.7%, and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio decreases to 161%. The forward FFO is 7.37, so the future expected FFO yield is around 13.56%. Pebblebrook Hotel Trust has a B credit rating from S&P. At the time of writing, the common stock of PEB is trading at a market price of $10.54:

Preferred stocks

PEB has four preferred stocks:

They are all trading below par at the moment, and none have a credit rating from the major credit agencies. The call dates of PEB.PR.E and PEB.PR.F have passed, so they are callable anytime. The call date of PEB.PR.G is on 05/13/2026, or it has 0.8 years to call, and PEB.PR.H has 1 year to call with a call date of 07/27/2026. All of PEB's preferred stocks have decent yields—over 8%.

(NYSE:PEB.PR.E) is priced $18.85. It has a current yield of 8.46%. It pays a cumulative distribution of 6.375% per annum, $1.59 annual amount, in the form of $0.3984375 per quarter.

At the time of writing, (NYSE:PEB.PR.F) has a market price of $19.20 and a current yield of 8.22%. It pays a cumulative distribution of 6.3% per annum, $1.58 annual amount, in the form of $0.39375 per quarter.

(NYSE:PEB.PR.G) is with a current price of $18.91 and a current yield of 8.43%. It pays a cumulative distribution of 6.38% per annum, $1.59375 annual amount, in the form of $0.3984375 per quarter.

(NYSE:PEB.PR.H)'s market price is $16.99, and its current yield is 8.47%. It pays a cumulative distribution of 5.70% per annum, $1.425 annual amount, in the form of $0.35625 per quarter.

OTC bonds

PEB has one unsecured bond:

It is rated by Moody's with a B2 credit rating. Its yield is average, around 6.3%.

Credit rating

We will follow Moody's approach and analyze each point in the picture, as many of our readers know:

  1. Gross assets: The total assets of the company are close to $5.65 billion, and this is a Baa2 credit rating according to Moody's.
  2. Unencumbered Assets/Gross Assets: Unencumbered Assets are 96% of the total assets—here Moody's rates with A1.
  3. Secured debt: The secured debt is close to 3% of the total assets—here we have an Aa3 equivalent credit score.
  4. Total debt + preferred stock/total assets: A ratio of around 39% leads to a Baa2 rating, indicates good financial health, and not so high leverage.
  5. Net debt to EBITDA: With total debt and EBITDA of $307 million and a ratio here of close to 9.42 (net debt/EBITDA), this metric results in a Caa2 credit rating.
  6. Fixed cost coverage: EBITDA covers the interest expenses and preferred dividend over 2 times, so the ratio here corresponds to a Ba2 credit rating.

After summing up all indicators, the unchanged combined score is 9.59, corresponding to a Baa3 credit rating. After applying adjustments, the value decreases to 10.15, corresponding to a Baa3 credit score again.

Market positioning and asset quality

Moody's rates hotels with a lower credit rating, market positioning, and operating environment rating, because they often operate in highly competitive locations. In addition, the sector is sensitive to economic downturns, seasonality, and fluctuations in demand, which increases risk for investors. This is why we give a PEB for market positioning and asset quality a credit rating of Caa3.

Operation environment

Here, the reason is the same, and the credit score too, Caa3.

Liquidity and access to capital

The company's operating expenses, excluding depreciation and amortization, are approximately 79% of revenue. EBITDA covers interest expenses and preferred stock dividends approximately twice. Based on these metrics, PEB receives a Baa3 rating. After taking into account assessments of market position, operating environment, and liquidity, as well as adjustments to all metrics for market conditions, the overall credit rating is Ba2. The preferred stock is rated with a rating that is two notches lower than this rating, or a B1 score.

Conclusion

PEB operates in a highly competitive environment, and the hotel sector is subject to economic fluctuations and seasonality. Because of these risks, its overall rating is lower, and the preferred shares receive a 2-notch downgrade in credit rating, despite having a decent yield of over 8%.

data.title: Mercantile Bank: With A LTD Ratio Of 100%, The Focus Is On The Securities Portfolio
data.description:
My last article on Mercantile Bank (NASDAQ:MBWM) was about a year ago, and I highlighted its ability to originate new loans and reduce its loan-to-deposit ratio. The latter was becoming a problem as it had exceeded 100%, but in Q2 2024, there were encouraging signs of a decline.

A year later, the loan-to-deposit ratio is below 100%, but the downward trend has not continued. The inability to increase deposits (especially cheap ones) probably remains the key issue for this bank to resolve.

Let's take a closer look at the most important aspects of Q2 2025.

Highlights Q2 2025

Mercantile Bank primarily deals with companies rather than retail customers, which is why commercial loans play a prominent role. In fact, C&I and Owner Occupied CRE together account for 55% of the loan portfolio.

This bank's typical loan is a variable-rate loan with a maturity of less than 12 months, so it is highly exposed to interest rate fluctuations. A more accommodating Fed than expected could significantly damage the profitability of its activities. Fixed-rate loans are a minority (22%), but we will look at the strategy the management is adopting to reduce the risk of interest rate cuts later on.

As in my last article, MBWM continues to operate in a market where there is strong demand for credit.

Mortgage loan origination is higher than in Q2 2024 and nearly exceeded Q3 2024 levels. This result is quite positive when viewed in the context of the current macroeconomic scenario. Interest rates are still very high, and the Fed seems reluctant to lower them due to a potential spike in inflation caused by tariffs. This is not the right scenario for those looking to take on debt, but demand for credit remains good nonetheless. However, there is a catch.

The loan-to-deposit ratio has risen back to about 100%, limiting the bank's operations: there is demand, but there is no capital to finance it. For this reason, the only way MBWM can make money is by selling its originations to other financial intermediaries, which is what it is doing.

It is earning millions of dollars from origination and sales, and only a small portion of these mortgages is retained on its balance sheet.

This is why average loans are growing very slowly. This image provided in MBWM's presentation is somewhat misleading because it appears that there has been strong growth, but in the end, we are only talking about 6.80% YoY.

Overall, MBWM has the demand to grow rapidly, but is not supported by deposit growth. This divergence has brought the loan-to-deposit ratio to around 100%, a threshold beyond which it is too risky to further increase the loan portfolio. Therefore, the company is focusing on originating loans and then selling them.

As I mentioned earlier, this bank is heavily exposed to variable rates and risks being hit hard if the Fed cuts rates more quickly. Its asset yields would plummet, and its net interest margin would shrink. Since it cannot work on its loan portfolio to solve this problem, the bank is focusing on its securities portfolio.

By purchasing fixed-rate securities, it can protect itself from falling rates without negatively impacting its capital ratios (the assets it purchases are mainly risk-free).

Most of the assets in the portfolio are US government agency bonds, and their position is growing larger every quarter. Furthermore, given that purchases are repeated and current market rates are high, the average yield is gradually increasing. It is currently above 2.50%, so it is still very low, but there is significant potential for growth.

Consider that within one year, bonds worth $50 million will mature with an average yield of only 1.02%; you could potentially reinvest this capital at a higher yield of 300-400 bps. In short, reinvestments and net purchases could bring the average yield above 3.50% over the next 12 months. This is a way to improve the net interest margin without weighing on the loan-to-deposit ratio.

The best way to solve this would clearly be to increase deposits without using expensive options, but management isn't able to do that right now.

Compared to last year, lower-cost deposits and non-interest-bearing deposits lost 1 and 2 percentage points, respectively, of total deposits. Conversely, higher-cost deposits increased by three percentage points, and brokered deposits remained unchanged.

The overall result of what we have seen so far is a slight increase in the net interest margin of 2 bps compared to the previous quarter, reaching 3.49%. The Investments segment performed the best, but this was fairly predictable given that it is currently the main focus of management.

Conclusion

Mercantile Bank is a solid bank that operates mainly with businesses and whose balance sheet would suffer from a faster-than-expected decline in interest rates. For this reason, management is investing in fixed-rate securities, and the strategy appears to be working. On the lending side, the demand is good but cannot be exploited to the full, given the high loan-to-deposit ratio.

Over the years, MBWM has demonstrated that it can grow faster than its peers and that it is a reliable bank. In addition, it also currently pays a highly sustainable dividend.

Its dividend yield (FWD) is 3.17%, and given that EPS far exceeds the dividend per share, I expect it to continue to grow by at least 5-6% per year, barring a recession. At the moment, there are no grounds for such pessimism, but it is worth noting that MBWM would suffer a severe blow, perhaps greater than the industry average. Being heavily weighted towards commercial loans, it is a highly cyclical bank with a relatively high-risk profile.

In 2008-2009, EPS fell sharply, and the dividend was cut.

However, the main reason for my hold rating is the current valuation.

The price-to-tangible book value ratio is slightly above the 3-year average, which prevents me from considering it a buy. The bank is not trading below its usual multiples, and the growth prospects are not so exciting as to make me believe that it deserves a higher multiple. With a multiple between 1.15x and 1.30x, I would be willing to revise my rating.

data.title: The New Axis Of Defense: Europe's Rise In Cyber, Sensors And Sovereignty
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA & Samuel Rines

Europe's defense resurgence isn't just about rearming—it's about reinventing. For decades, Europe relied on a post–Cold War peace dividend that allowed defense innovation to languish on the continent while attention shifted elsewhere.1 That era is over. What's rising in its place is a deeply strategic push into next-generation technologies: cyber platforms built for offensive and defensive maneuvering, artificial intelligence (AI)-integrated surveillance and targeting systems, secure communication layers and resilient supply chains. This isn't an arms race—it's a technological transformation. And crucially, it's not driven by budget cycles alone but by the hard lessons of Ukraine, the tailwinds of digital sovereignty and a new understanding that defense in the 21st century is won as much with software as with steel.

U.S. investors are just beginning to notice the shift. Europe's defense landscape is fragmenting and reforming in a pattern that looks far more like a startup ecosystem than a legacy industrial base. National champions like Leonardo and Thales are fusing legacy platforms with cutting-edge cyber assets. Quietly, smaller players—particularly in Finland, Germany, Italy and the Baltics—are becoming indispensable links in Europe's evolving defense tech stack. This is where the opportunity lies: in recognizing that Europe's next generation of defense is not simply an expansion of what already exists but a re-platforming of what defense even means. When the world's defense priorities shift toward autonomy, data resilience and AI-driven decision cycles, Europe's next-generation edge could become the core of a global investment thesis.

From Defense Electronics to Digital Dominance

If there's one company that epitomizes Europe's redefinition of defense, it's Thales. Long seen as a quiet powerhouse behind radar systems, avionics and encrypted communications, Thales has become the cornerstone of Europe's digital shield. With roots in military electronics, the company has leaned hard into cyber, doubling down through acquisitions like Imperva and Tesserent, and quadrupling its cybersecurity revenues since 2016.2 Now ranked among the top five global players in cyber defense, Thales anchors sovereign cloud infrastructure, zero-trust architecture3 and AI-enabled threat intelligence for the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), the European Union (EU) and critical global enterprises. And this isn't aspirational—it's operational. In 2024 alone, Cyber & Digital sales rose nearly 15%, while EBIT4 margins in that segment hit an enviable 14.5%.5

But Thales is not just scaling cyber revenues—it's defining what modern defense looks like. Nearly €4.2 billion annually is pumped into R&D—20% of the company's entire sales base—funding everything from autonomous maritime drones to AI-powered data risk platforms. Their internal cortAIx initiative, now home to 800 AI experts and 100+ integrated products, is building the trusted AI layer for military and critical infrastructure across Europe.6 For investors, this is where the signal rises above the noise: Thales is not a niche cyber play or a legacy defense contractor—it's the connective tissue of European security, fusing software, sensors and systems into a tech-centric moat. With guidance for 5%–7% annual organic sales growth and record-high orders of €25.3 billion in 2024, Thales is building one of the most durable and defensible franchises in European defense tech.7

Figure 1: Trustworthy AI and Tactical Tech: Thales' Innovation Portfolio

Leonardo: Italy's Defense Giant Goes Digital

For most U.S. investors, Leonardo is a name that rings faintly, if at all. Yet in Europe, it's the backbone of Italy's defense-industrial complex and a major player across aerospace, helicopters and secure communications. What's changing—and fast—is the company's DNA. Leonardo is no longer just building hardware; it's embedding itself into the software layer of 21st-century defense. In 2024, the company generated over €4 billion in revenue from its Cyber & Security Solutions division and announced a multi-year roadmap to double that figure.8 That plan includes targeted acquisitions of smaller cybersecurity firms and aggressive expansion in digital command-and-control infrastructure—an area where digital sovereignty and NATO interoperability are converging into a new must-have standard.

The shift is not cosmetic. Leonardo's cyber push is central to its transformation into a full-spectrum aerospace, defense and security provider. It's backing that ambition with real capital: in July 2025, it acquired a 24.55% stake in Finland's SSH Communications Security, a specialist in quantum-safe encryption and privileged access management—technologies that will likely define the next decade of defense-grade network architecture.9 The company's roadmap points to a cyber division growing at a 14.4% CAGR, with EBITA10 forecasted to quadruple by 2028.11 What Thales is to Franco-European cyber infrastructure, Leonardo is fast becoming for southern Europe—a digital anchor tied to real hardware, trusted by governments and strategically built to secure the future battlefield.

Figure 2: Doubling Down on Digital: Leonardo's Cyber Trajectory

Saab: From Cars to Counter-UAS—Sweden's Tech-Forward Defense Champion

Most U.S. investors still associate the name "Saab" with quirky Scandinavian cars. But the brand exited the auto business over a decade ago, and what emerged is one of Europe's most advanced, software-integrated defense platforms. With Sweden and Finland now in NATO, Saab's order book is surging—its SEK 197.6 billion backlog reflects growing global demand for electronic warfare suites, networked sensors and advanced air defense systems.12 The company has ramped up investment in "software-defined" and AI-enabled defense infrastructure, including new capabilities in collaborative autonomous drones (Gnadd), passive EW systems (Sirius Compact) and AI-based command and control architectures.

If you're looking for a case study in how shifting geopolitical realities translate into corporate performance, Saab is writing a textbook in real time. The backdrop couldn't be clearer: NATO's post-summit commitment to 5% of gross domestic product (GDP) defense spending by 2035 is no longer theoretical—it's becoming operational.13 Sweden, now a NATO member, is putting SEK 300 billion behind that shift.14 Saab finds itself not just participating in this environment but increasingly defining the categories that matter: long-range surveillance, multi-domain deterrence and aerial sovereignty. The GlobalEye and Gripen E/F platforms are now being discussed not just with Thailand and Colombia, but as part of NATO interoperability frameworks, with France publicly aligning on future procurements. You don't get into those conversations unless your product is not just good, but uniquely valuable. That's what makes Saab's order intake so telling—SEK 28.4 billion this quarter, skewed toward small- and medium-sized deals.15 That's how you know the demand is real and urgent. Countries aren't waiting. They're buying now.

And Saab is executing on the opportunity. Organic revenue up 32%—yes, that's the real number. Dynamics posted 73% top-line growth, with EBIT margins leaping to 20.9%, and Surveillance wasn't far behind with 21% growth and margin expansion to 9.0%. What's powering that isn't just sales, but throughput. They're delivering systems, not just talking about them. It's worth pausing on what that does to the P&L: EBIT up 49%, margins to 10.0%, and a full-year sales growth guide raised to 16%–20%. Importantly, they're doing this while ramping up R&D—SEK 1.7 billion in H1—and still improving profitability.16 They ran their first AI-enabled Gripen test flight this quarter. Their counter-drone solution landed its first export deal. These aren't speculative R&D projects. They're live. Cash flow was negative, but for good reasons: capacity buildouts, inventory to meet future demand, and a supply chain being proactively fortified. Returns on capital and equity rose to 15.3% and 14.0%, respectively.17 This isn't a quarter you celebrate. It's one you build from. Saab knows where defense is headed, and it's moving there with speed and precision.

Hensoldt: Germany's Eyes and Ears of the New Battlefield

If Europe's rearmament has a neural network, Hensoldt is the signal processor behind it. Spun off from Airbus in 2017, Hensoldt has become Germany's national champion in radar, electromagnetic warfare and battle-proven sensor integration. It's a company that operates in the shadows—developing the critical sensing, jamming and decision-support layers that make 21st-century defense systems intelligent, interoperable and resilient. With over €6.929 billion in backlog, Hensoldt has hit structural growth velocity, driven by radar production, integrated optronics and secure electronic intelligence systems that underpin Europe's multi-domain operations.18 It's also one of the few defense tech companies explicitly architecting its strategy around "software-defined defence"—a term that, in Hensoldt's case, isn't marketing speak, but a business model centered on data, modularity and sensor fusion.

The company's role isn't about volume—it's about informational dominance. Whether through advanced radar for Eurofighters and future combat air systems, secure communication layers for tactical edge units or cyber defense infrastructure to shield critical sensor data, Hensoldt is playing the long game in defense autonomy and information superiority. Its growth is methodical and margin-rich: Hensoldt delivered nearly €405 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2024, with over 18% margins and cash conversion in the 50%–60% range. With demand accelerating across NATO for modular, cyber-hardened ISR and C4ISR components, Hensoldt's path toward €5 billion in revenue by 2030—up from €2.2 billion in 2024—isn't speculative, it's strategic.19 In short: this is one of the most critical and underrecognized players in the race for digital sovereignty in European defense.

Conclusion: The Strategic Frontier Few Are Watching

Europe is not just catching up in defense—it's leapfrogging into domains that will define the next era of deterrence. Investors conditioned to focus on U.S. primes and traditional platforms may be missing a far more dynamic story: a continent forced by geopolitical urgency to rethink sovereignty, resilience and how software integrates with steel. What's emerging is a new industrial layer—one powered by secure sensors, cyber-immune networks, AI-augmented decision cycles and high-value integration across land, air, sea, space and data. These aren't just defense companies anymore—they're critical infrastructure builders for a new era of multipolar conflict and digital warfare.

Thales, Leonardo, Saab and Hensoldt are building the digital backbone of NATO's evolving posture. The WisdomTree Europe Defense Fund (WDEF), which is tracking the total return performance of the WisdomTree Europe Defense Index, has exposure to all four of these firms and provides an option for U.S. investors seeking to gain exposure to what we believe is a defining global feature of the coming decade, and perhaps even longer.

Figure 3: Exposure in WDEF to the Companies Discussed in This Piece

Footnotes

1 Source: "The Costly End of Europe's ‘Peace Dividend,'" Financial Times, 3/17/25.

2 Source: R. Throsby, "France's Thales Sees Revenue and Profit Growth after Cyber Expansion," Reuters, 11/14/24.

3 Zero trust architecture in cybersecurity is a security model based on the principle, "Never trust, always verify."

4 EBIT refers to earnings before interest and taxes.

5 Source: Thales Group, "Thales Reports its 2024 Full-Year Results," thalesgroup.com, 3/4/25.

6 Sources: Thales Group, "Thales Builds on Four Key Strengths: €4.2 Billion in R&D, Around 800 AI Experts, Over 20,000 Patents," PublicNow, June 2024; Thales Group, "Thales Speeds Up R&D on AI for Defence: Launch of cortAIx AI Accelerator," Aviation Defence Universe, 3/29/24; Thales Group, "cortAIx SG: Thales Accelerates Trusted AI Innovation in Singapore," thalesgroup.com, 6/2/25.

7 Source: R. Throsby, "France's Thales Sees Revenue and Profit Growth after Cyber Expansion," Reuters, 11/14/24.

8 Source: Leonardo S.p.A., "Board of Directors Approves FY 2024 Results and 2025 Guidance," leonardo.com, 3/11/25.

9 Source: "Italy's Leonardo Becomes Biggest Shareholder of Finland's SSH with Stake Purchase," Reuters, 7/1/25.

10 EBITA refers to earnings before interest, taxes and amortization.

11 Source: "Leonardo: Industrial Plan Update – Cyber Division Growth Guidance," EDR Magazine, 3/11/25.

12 Source: Saab AB, "Q2 2025 Interim Report: Accelerating Growth and Strengthening our Market Position," saab.com, 7/18/25.

13 Source: North Atlantic Treaty Organization, "NATO Concludes Historic Summit in The Hague," nato.int, 6/25/25.

14 Source: Government of Sweden, "CrossParty Agreement on Historic Rearmament," government.se, 6/19/25.

15 Source: Saab AB, "Q2 2025 Interim Report: Accelerating Growth and Strengthening our Market Position," saab.com, 7/18/25.

16 Source: Saab AB, "Q2 2025 Interim Report: Accelerating Growth and Strengthening our Market Position," saab.com, 7/18/25.

17 Source: Saab AB, "Q2 2025 Interim Report: Accelerating Growth and Strengthening our Market Position," saab.com, 7/18/25.

18 Source: HENSOLDT AG, "HENSOLDT Reports Strong First Quarter 2025 with Growth in Order Intake and Revenue," hensoldt.net, 5/7/25.

19 Source: HENSOLDT AG, "HENSOLDT Achieves Record Order Backlog in Financial Year 2024 – Guidance for All Key Figures Met, Partly Exceeded," hensoldt.net, 2/27/25.

Important Risks Related to this Article

For current holdings of WDEF, please click here. Holdings are subject to risk and change.

There are risks associated with investing, including the potential loss of principal. Foreign investing involves specific risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. This Fund focuses its investments in Europe, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments in Europe that can adversely affect performance. Europe has and may continue to experience security concerns, war, threats of war, aggression and/or conflict, terrorism, economic uncertainty, sanctions or the threat of sanctions, natural and environmental disasters, the spread of infectious illness, widespread disease or other public health issues and/or systemic market dislocations that lead to increased short-term market volatility and have adverse long-term effects on European and world economies and disrupt the orderly functioning of securities markets generally, which may negatively impact the Fund’s investments. Many countries within Europe are closely connected and their economies and markets are largely interdependent. As such, economic and political events in one European country, including monetary exchange rates between European countries and armed conflicts among two or more European countries, may have adverse effects across Europe. European countries that are members of the European Union (“EU”) and the European Economic and Monetary Union (“EMU”) are subject to certain economic and monetary policies and controls and the risks associated with such coordinated economic and fiscal policies. Because the Fund invests primarily in the securities of companies in Europe, the Fund’s performance is expected to be closely tied to social, political and economic conditions within Europe and to be more volatile than the performance of more geographically diversified funds. Investments in non-U.S. securities involve political, regulatory and economic risks that may not be present in investments in U.S. securities. The securities of small-capitalization companies generally trade in lower volumes and are subject to greater and more unpredictable price changes than larger-capitalization stocks or the stock market as a whole. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index. The Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Samuel Rines, Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Prior to WisdomTree, Samuel was a Managing Director of CORBU, a research firm we struck up a relationship with to deliver model portfolios. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book “After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy”.

data.title: Weyerhaeuser Company 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Weyerhaeuser Company in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.
